title: OKRs

content.statement: Align the organization around a few ambitious objectives, each with measurable key results.
content.question: What 3 objectives, with measurable key results, should you commit to publicly this quarter?
content.equation: Aligned Output = Objective Clarity × KR Measurability × Public Commitment
content.mental_model: North star with a checklist, direction plus measurable progress.
content.business_lens: Each cycle, define 3–5 objectives with 3–4 measurable key results; review weekly; treat 0.7 score as healthy.
content.life_lens: Pick a few audacious goals each quarter and define how you will measure if you got there.
sop: Reset each cycle and learn from misses
content.explainer: Google’s OKRs powered its early growth: bold objectives like "improve search quality" with hard KRs like "increase query-success rate." OKRs are not bonus systems, tying KRs to compensation kills stretch. The point is alignment and learning.
questions: What 3 objectives, with measurable key results, should you commit to publicly this quarter?
Which of your current goals has no actual number attached to know if you hit it?
Would your team be embarrassed or motivated if your scores were posted publicly right now?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Objective Clarity 8, Key Result Measurability 7, Public Commitment 6. Score = 8 times 7 times 6 = 336 out of a possible 1,000. Higher means the whole team can explain what winning looks like without asking you. Lower means the objectives exist on a slide but not in anyone's actual weekly work.
content.closing_statement: A goal without a number is just a wish.

caption:
Principle Nº 284: OKRs

Align the organization around a few ambitious objectives, each with measurable key results.

Ask yourself: What 3 objectives, with measurable key results, should you commit to publicly this quarter?
